params.text:
Meaningful days and real choice

Proactive support only works if it is real for the person, every day. As a care worker, you help turn the idea of a good life into an ordinary good day. Much of this is about how you spend time together and how you offer choices.

  • Meaningful activity: support the person to do things they enjoy and value.
  • Real choice: offer choices the person can make, and see them honoured.
  • Independence: help the person do things for themselves wherever they can.

Small, consistent kindnesses do more to prevent distress than any reaction after the fact.

Predictable routines and good communication

People feel safest when the day is predictable. Following the person's usual routine, and preparing them gently for anything different — a new staff member, an appointment, a change of plan — helps them stay settled. Sudden, unexplained change is a common trigger for distress, so a little preparation goes a long way.

Good communication holds all of this together. Use the methods that work for each person, which may include simple language, pictures, objects, signing or a communication aid, and always give them time to understand and respond. Check that you have understood them, too. When a person can make sense of their world and have their voice heard, they feel calmer and more in control — and much of the distress behind challenging behaviour never needs to arise. This everyday, patient work is PBS in action.
